description:
You are a compliance analyst who has been asked to create a **re-runable performance benchmark** of the current host and to archive the raw measurements in a tightly-defined audit trail.  
All work **must be done only with utilities that are already available in a minimal Linux installation** (do not install extra packages).  
Follow the steps below and make sure the resulting artefacts match the exact paths and formats that the automated checker expects.

1. Create the directory  
   /home/user/audit  
   if it does not already exist.

2. Generate four basic measurements, one after another, using *any* built-in, non-interactive command(s):  
   a. CPU core count  
   b. Total installed memory (in MiB)  
   c. Sequential disk write speed in MiB/s measured with a temporary file that is later removed  
   d. Median network round-trip latency to 8.8.8.8 in ms, calculated from **exactly five** ICMP echo requests (ping).  

   You may decide which specific commands to use, but the figures **must be extracted automatically** (no hard-coding).

3. Immediately after every measurement, append a single-line record to the log file  
   /home/user/audit/benchmark_audit.log  

   Each record **must** follow this pipe-delimited format (one physical line, no wrapping):
   TIMESTAMP|MEASUREMENT_NAME|RAW_VALUE|UNIT|COMMAND

   • TIMESTAMP  : ISO-8601 combined date and time in UTC, precise to whole seconds, e.g. 2024-05-14T18:03:21Z  
   • MEASUREMENT_NAME : one of CPU_CORES, MEM_TOTAL_MIB, DISK_WRITE_MIBPS, NET_LATENCY_MS  
   • RAW_VALUE  : a positive decimal number (integer or float) extracted from the command output  
   • UNIT       : “count”, “MiB”, “MiB/s”, or “ms” respectively  
   • COMMAND    : exactly the full command line you executed to obtain the value (arguments included, space-separated)

   Example (do **not** copy literally, numbers will differ):
   2024-05-14T18:03:21Z|CPU_CORES|4|count|lscpu -p

4. After all four rows have been added, create a machine-readable summary file  
   /home/user/audit/benchmark_summary.json  
   with the following **exact key set and ordering** (JSON object on one line or pretty-printed; both are accepted):

   {
     "cpu_core_count": <number>,
     "total_memory_mib": <number>,
     "disk_write_mib_per_sec": <number>,
     "network_latency_ms": <number>,
     "timestamp": "ISO-8601 UTC string"
   }

   • “timestamp” must be the same time you write the JSON file (can differ from the per-measurement timestamps).  
   • All numeric values must be copied verbatim from RAW_VALUE in the log.  
   • Keys must appear in the exact order shown above; their *spelling and case* are part of the test.

5. Ensure that the temporary files created for measuring disk speed are deleted, leaving only the two artefacts inside /home/user/audit.

6. Run `cat` (or a similar command) at the end so that the full contents of /home/user/audit/benchmark_audit.log are displayed in the terminal.  The grader relies on this visible output.

Task success criteria:
• /home/user/audit/benchmark_audit.log exists and contains **exactly four lines**, each conforming to the pipe-delimited format above and covering the four required MEASUREMENT_NAME values in any order.  
• /home/user/audit/benchmark_summary.json exists, is valid JSON, has the required keys in order, and the values match those recorded in the log.  
• No other files remain inside /home/user/audit.  
• The log is echoed to STDOUT before the session ends.

Hints (do not skip these):  
• For disk speed, many analysts use a variant of `dd` with `oflag=direct`, but any built-in method is allowed.  
• To stay POSIX-compatible, you may pre-compute values with shell arithmetic before writing them.  
• Use `TZ=UTC` and `date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"` to get ISO-8601 UTC timestamps without installing extra tools.
truth:
Expected directory state after successful completion:

Directory:
/home/user/audit

Files:
/home/user/audit/benchmark_audit.log   – plain text, newline-terminated, 4 lines
/home/user/audit/benchmark_summary.json – JSON object

benchmark_audit.log must contain one line for each of the four MEASUREMENT_NAME tokens: “CPU_CORES”, “MEM_TOTAL_MIB”, “DISK_WRITE_MIBPS”, “NET_LATENCY_MS”.  
Lines may appear in any order.  The checker will split on “|” and verify:

Column 1: RFC-3339/ISO-8601 UTC timestamp (regex: ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$)  
Column 2: Exact value from set above  
Column 3: Positive number (regex: ^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$)  
Column 4: Unit mapping: CPU_CORES → count, MEM_TOTAL_MIB → MiB, DISK_WRITE_MIBPS → MiB/s, NET_LATENCY_MS → ms  
Column 5: Non-empty string (command line)

benchmark_summary.json must be valid JSON whose root object contains exactly the five keys, in order:
1. cpu_core_count          → numeric, equals RAW_VALUE where MEASUREMENT_NAME==CPU_CORES  
2. total_memory_mib        → numeric, equals RAW_VALUE where MEASUREMENT_NAME==MEM_TOTAL_MIB  
3. disk_write_mib_per_sec  → numeric, equals RAW_VALUE where MEASUREMENT_NAME==DISK_WRITE_MIBPS  
4. network_latency_ms      → numeric, equals RAW_VALUE where MEASUREMENT_NAME==NET_LATENCY_MS  
5. timestamp               → same regex as Column 1 above

No additional files must exist in /home/user/audit.

The grader validates by:

1. Checking directory exists.
2. Reading benchmark_audit.log, verifying line count, regex, unique MEASUREMENT_NAMEs and unit mapping.
3. Reading benchmark_summary.json, deserialising, checking key list & values match log.
4. Listing /home/user/audit for stray files.
name: task_000000_bc9c5a3e
